title: NYU Langone Visiting International Physicians Program — Orthopedic Surgery
shortDescription: Sponsor-arranged academic observership for international orthopedic physicians at NYU Langone's Department of Orthopedic Surgery.
fullDescription: NYU Langone's Department of Orthopedic Surgery runs an academic observership designed for international physicians who want direct exposure to the techniques and clinical workflow of one of the nation's high-volume orthopedic programs. The program is sponsor-driven: applicants must first identify a faculty host inside the department, then work with that sponsor to agree on the length of the visit, the physical location (hospital, outpatient site, or OR block), and the specific clinical activities before any paperwork moves. Sandra Martinez, Project Associate, coordinates the process and funnels packets to both the Department and NYU Langone's Chief Medical Officer for final approval. Eligibility requires a medical degree; the page does not list USMLE, ECFMG, or graduation-year requirements. Fees are not posted. Visa support, letters of recommendation, and certificate issuance are not addressed publicly, so applicants should ask directly when initiating the process. Applicants submit a CV and a formal letter of interest. Like NYU's other international observerships, this is structured as shadowing and academic exchange rather than hands-on training, so US residency Match relevance is limited.
duration: Flexible; set by applicant and faculty sponsor
eligibilitySummary: MD required; faculty sponsor inside NYU Orthopedics required. No USMLE/ECFMG/graduation criteria published.

title: NYU Langone Academic Observership — Otolaryngology (ENT)
shortDescription: Faculty-sponsored ENT observership capped at 30 days; lectures, tumor boards, and clinic/OR observation.
fullDescription: The Department of Otolaryngology-Head & Neck Surgery at NYU Langone hosts international physicians through a short academic observership capped at a strict 30-day maximum. Admitted observers sit in on didactic educational lectures, grand rounds, and tumor boards, and may also observe clinical practice in the office setting and surgical procedures in the operating room. The program is explicit that participants are not considered students, residents, fellows, or trainees, and no academic credit or certificate is awarded on completion — a notable difference from the dermatology tracks. Eligibility is framed around physicians from around the world with strong English comprehension; USMLE, ECFMG, and graduation-year constraints are not listed, but a faculty sponsor inside the department is mandatory. International applicants must obtain either a B-1 visa or visa waiver, and must clear NYU Langone's HR Immigration Office after final sign-off from the Chief Medical Officer. Applicants submit the Academic Observer Agreement form along with CV, proof of advanced degrees, and government-issued photo ID, and must complete vaccinations and screenings before activity begins. Because there is no certificate, no LOR commitment, and an explicit 'not a trainee' disclaimer, the rotation is best viewed as specialty-visiting exposure.
eligibilitySummary: MD implied; strong English required; faculty sponsor from ENT required. B-1 visa or waiver required. No USMLE/ECFMG criteria published.

title: NYU Langone Observer Program — Plastic Surgery
shortDescription: One-month plastic surgery observer rotation: OR, clinic, and conferences across six subspecialty areas.
fullDescription: NYU Langone's Hansjörg Wyss Department of Plastic Surgery runs a tightly scoped observer program with a hard one-month cap set by institutional policy. Visiting physicians and advanced-degree professionals attend clinical sessions, lectures, and conferences, and observe surgeons in the OR across microsurgery, breast reconstruction, aesthetic surgery, craniofacial surgery, wound care, and hand surgery. Eligible participants hold an MD, DDS, research doctorate, or physician assistant certification, and must arrive with a government-issued photo ID and a signed letter of recommendation from their chair or department head. USMLE, ECFMG, and graduation-year thresholds are not published. Applicants send a single consolidated PDF (CV, chair LOR, degree copy, ID, health documentation) to administrative coordinator Jenny Castaneda-Nakouzi. Approval flows through the department chair and NYU Langone's CMO. The department frames the program as enhancing specialty knowledge rather than feeding the US residency Match: the public page explicitly states observers do not receive academic credit and are not considered trainees. Visas are the applicant's responsibility — NYU Langone requires a B-1 visa or visa waiver, does not sponsor J-1, and does not issue a certificate on completion.
eligibilitySummary: MD, DDS, research doctorate, or PA certification. Signed LOR from chair required. B-1 visa or waiver required.
graduationYearPref: Fully trained specialists; 1st/2nd-year residents and recent graduates ineligible

title: NYU Rusk Rehabilitation Physician Observership
shortDescription: PM&R observership at NYU Rusk for international physiatrists and PM&R residents — international medical students not accepted.
fullDescription: Rusk Rehabilitation at NYU Langone is one of the largest academic rehabilitation networks in the United States, and its Physician Observership Program is targeted specifically at rehabilitation physicians who want enhanced exposure to Rusk's inpatient and outpatient PM&R practice. The program is explicit about its audience: international physicians, residents enrolled in accredited PM&R programs in their home countries, and practicing physiatrists. International medical students are not accommodated under this track. Rusk runs a separate, non-compensated PT/OT observation pathway for allied-health students. Applicants must complete the Rusk application, supply supporting documents, and submit a TOEFL score of at least 72. USMLE, ECFMG, and graduation-year requirements are not published, and the page does not disclose fee, duration, visa sponsorship, or LOR availability. A certificate of completion is issued at the end, though the program does not award educational credits. Applicants should contact Gladys Obenewa, Senior Program Coordinator, early to obtain fee structure and scheduling clarity.
eligibilitySummary: International physiatrists or PM&R residents only. International medical students excluded. Minimum TOEFL 72.

title: Memorial Sloan Kettering International Observership
shortDescription: Short-form oncology observership at MSK for fully trained international physicians — explicitly not for IMG residency applicants.
fullDescription: Memorial Sloan Kettering's International Observership is a short, tightly bounded program that lets fully trained international physicians shadow MSK faculty across clinical and surgical oncology services for a maximum of 30 days, with program start dates every other Monday. Applicants must be physicians with several years of practice currently employed abroad, or third- or fourth-year residents abroad during their fellowship year with documented proof. The program is unusually explicit about who it is not for: recent medical graduates, first- and second-year junior residents, medical students, and — crucially — international medical graduates residing in the US or abroad who intend to apply for US residency or fellowship. The page states the program is 'not designed' for those applicants. The application model is distinctive: MSK does not match applicants to faculty, so candidates must first identify and contact an MSK faculty member directly. That faculty member submits the official request six months in advance; the applicant then completes MSK's online application. No certificate or academic credit is awarded — the page states unambiguously: 'This is an Observership program only.' Federal regulations bar international observers from any patient care responsibilities or volunteer research. Visitors handle their own visas.
duration: Up to 30 days; biweekly Monday starts
cost: Not stated; observer covers all expenses including visa
eligibilitySummary: Fully trained physicians or 3rd/4th-year residents abroad only. Explicitly excludes junior residents, students, and IMGs pursuing US residency/fellowship.
graduationYearPref: Fully trained specialists; 1st/2nd-year residents and recent graduates ineligible

page_excerpts.text:
The Observership Program is fully booked for period from Sept 2026-Feb 2027
No further applications will be accepted at this time.
The application portal will re-open from March 22, 2027 to April 23, 2027 to accept applications for the period from September 2027- June 2028. (There are no observerships offered in July/August).

Please note: due to the timeline of our application cycle, we strongly recommend that those who are planning to apply for residency in the US should apply to our observership program at least a year in advance.

The Department of Psychiatry at the New York State Psychiatric Institute and Columbia University Medical Center offers observership (shadowing) opportunities for international medical students and graduates of international medical training programs. The goal of the program is to provide participants with exposure to the American psychiatric healthcare system and its clinical practices.

The Clinical Observership Program spans four weeks and follows a rotating structure. During the program, participants spend one week each on four different clinical services. These may include areas such as an inpatient unit, the psychiatric emergency room, a consultation service, or a child psychiatry service. Please note that the specific services assigned cannot be confirmed in advance.

There is a fee of $2000. Housing is not provided. We do not sponsor any visas.
We do not offer research observerships.

Curriculum Vitae: Please include an updated version of your CV as part of this application.
Letters of Reference: Three letters of references from individuals who are closely acquainted with your educational and/or work experience. At least one letter should be from your Medical School or Training Director.
Personal Statement: Please write a single-spaced essay of no fewer than 400 words and no more than 800 words on why you are applying to this program.

After the application is complete, a video-interview will be offered to discuss your interests, your ability to communicate in English, and the role of the observership in your career plans. In general, clinical observerships are 4 weeks long and begin on Mondays throughout the year. We usually can only host 10 observers each academic year.

Margaret Hamilton MD — Director of International Medical Students and Graduates Rotations — Email: mis3@cumc.columbia.edu
Karen Cruz — Program Coordinator — Email: Karen.Cruz@nyspi.columbia.edu — Phone: (646) 774-7505

title: UAB International Visiting Medical Observership
shortDescription: UAB Heersink structured observership for recent IMGs with published USMLE Step 1 requirement and LOR in application.
fullDescription: The University of Alabama at Birmingham Heersink School of Medicine runs one of the more structured fee-based observerships in the Southeast US. The program is built for recent international medical graduates holding an MD or equivalent (MBBS) as well as international medical students, with an explicit USMLE Step 1 passing score required at application and USMLE Step 2/3 preferred but optional. English proficiency is verified via TOEFL or OET Medicine minimum scores unless the applicant qualifies for a waiver. The application packet includes the medical diploma, CV, one letter of recommendation, and a personal statement, packaged as two PDFs and submitted through an online portal at least three months before the desired start. Fees are non-trivial and clearly posted: $350 non-refundable application fee plus $4,250 per four-week slot; 8-week engagements are two consecutive 4-week slots. Observers additionally pay for TB chest X-ray, housing, transport, and document at least $2,000 of living expenses. UAB does not sponsor visas — a B-1/B-2 with a B-1 (Business) stamp is recommended. The program is strict about scope: no direct or indirect patient interaction, no hands-on training, shadowing of faculty, attendings, and chief residents only, with attendance at daily rounds, Grand Rounds, teaching rounds, journal clubs, and conferences. UAB publicly states the observership 'does not have any bearing on pending/future residency nor fellowship applications.' Because UAB accepts IMGs, requires USMLE Step 1, is an academic medical center, and the application includes a letter of recommendation, it is a strong Tier A candidate.
eligibilitySummary: Recent IMG (MD/MBBS) or international medical student. USMLE Step 1 required; Step 2/3 preferred. TOEFL/OET required unless waived. ECFMG is a TOEFL-waiver pathway. One LOR in packet. B-1/B-2 visa required.
graduationYearPref: Recent international medical graduates or current international medical students
stepRequirements: USMLE Step 1 passing score required; Step 2/3 preferred

title: NAMC Internal Medicine Observership
shortDescription: Low-fee internal medicine observership tied to the NAMC IM residency — 2026 slots are FULL; 2027 applications open October 1, 2026.
fullDescription: NAMC runs a low-fee internal medicine observership positioned alongside its Internal Medicine Residency program. Eligibility on the public page is unusually terse: applicants must have already passed USMLE Step 2 exams. The fee structure is notably modest — $250 payable by check or cash only — which stands out against peer programs charging four-figure monthly tuition. The application packet is substantial despite the low price: Observership Application, Indemnification Form, Confidentiality Statement, Fit Testing Disclaimer, COVID-19 Exemption Form, and IT Access Form. Total processing takes up to 45 days. Current-year availability is closed — all 2026 observership slots have been filled, and no additional 2026 applications will be accepted. The program is also blacked out for observer starts in June, July, and August each year. Applications for the 2027 cycle do not open until October 1, 2026. Because the program is directly affiliated with an IM residency and explicitly requires USMLE Step 2 before application, it is intrinsically positioned for IMG Match applicants.

title: UCLA Health International Physician Observership
shortDescription: UCLA Health hosts international physician observers in one- to three-month rotations — no LORs and no bearing on residency per UCLA policy.
fullDescription: UCLA Health's International Physician Observership runs out of the David Geffen School of Medicine's international services office and places international physicians into clinical host departments for one to three months, with longer stays only when the host department approves. The program is fee-heavy: a $750 non-refundable application fee plus full self-funding for travel, housing, and living. UCLA's scope disclosures are unusually direct. Observers cannot participate in patient care, cannot advise on cases, have no access to UCLA's CareConnect EMR, no library or email credentials, and cannot participate in volunteer research. The whole program is shadowing only. Two statements on the page materially affect the Match-credibility classification: UCLA Health explicitly declines to provide any LOR regarding observer participation, and it states that participation 'has no bearing on pending or future residency or fellowship applications.' Entry requires a medical degree from an accredited school, English proficiency, and a B-1 visa; UCLA does not assist with visas. Applicants must first obtain acceptance from a host department, then complete the online application. UCLA does issue a Certificate of Participation. Because the program explicitly refuses LORs and disclaims residency value, it is classified as Specialty Visiting / Tier C regardless of UCLA's prestige.
eligibilitySummary: Medical degree required. English proficiency required. B-1 visa mandatory. UCLA explicitly refuses LORs and disclaims residency-application value.

title: UCSF Neuropathology Visiting Scholars Program
shortDescription: UCSF neuropathology observership open to national and international graduates; attendance record only, no certificate.
fullDescription: The UCSF Department of Pathology runs a visiting scholars program in neuropathology that welcomes medical students, graduates, and practicing physicians from the US and abroad. Scholars rotate through the neuropathology service and may arrange time across multiple faculty members depending on their specific interests. Typical stays last between one and three months, providing exposure to surgical neuropathology, autopsy neuropathology, and subspecialty conferences. The program is strictly observational; no direct patient care or hands-on work is permitted. UCSF is transparent about what scholars receive: no stipend is offered, the program does not issue a formal completion certificate, and faculty state plainly that the rotation is usually too short to generate a residency letter of recommendation. Scholars leave with an attendance record documenting the time spent. Applicants are responsible for their own visa, immunizations, and administrative fees; the program does not sponsor visas. The value here is the brand, mentorship, and content exposure of a top academic neuropathology service rather than Match-credentialing documents.
cost: No fee; observer covers visa, immunizations, admin costs

auditData.title: UCSF Neuropathology Visiting Scholars Program  [CA · San Francisco]

page_excerpts.text:
Neuropathology Visiting Scholars Program — Overview
The Neuropathology Division at UCSF School of Medicine is one of the largest in the country and welcomes observers at all levels of neuropathology expertise as long as we can establish realistic, reasonable and attainable goals for their visit. Our division follows the noble tradition of late Prof. Bernd W. Scheithauer in opening our doors to students at all levels who will be practicing neuropathology and passing on the knowledge they acquired to others around the world.
The Neuropathology Observership Program was established to assist national and international students and medical graduates who wish to observe the neuropathology practice at UCSF. The program should acculturate students at all levels to UCSF pathology practices and help prepare them for further training. Terms typically last from one to three months, and the observer can rotate among several teaching faculty.
Neuropathology Observership Program is not organized for profit. Faculty members and staff volunteer their time and efforts. Actual costs (visa and administrative fees, immunizations, etc.) must be paid for by the observer. No salary or stipend can be provided for any observership. We cannot provide or issue a certificate and it is often not long enough to provide you with a letter of recommendation for residency or job applications, so please do not be offended if we cannot provide you with nothing but an attendance record and a remarkable memory of life in our division.

title: International Trainee Pediatric Observership — CU Anschutz / Children's Colorado
shortDescription: One-month pediatric observership at CU Anschutz / Children's Colorado requiring completed Step 1 and Step 2.
fullDescription: The Department of Pediatrics at the University of Colorado School of Medicine, together with Children's Hospital Colorado, operates a structured observership for international pediatric or family-medicine trainees and physicians. Rotations are fixed at one month, with July and December blacked out and availability rolling roughly eight months ahead. Observers attend inpatient rounds, ambulatory clinics, and departmental conferences under the continuous supervision of an attending, fellow, or supervising resident; they are not permitted to take histories, examine patients, write notes or orders, or access the EMR. Applications are submitted through a Qualtrics portal to coordinator Michael Baca and must include a CV, medical-school transcript, and a faculty letter describing class rank and honors. Importantly, the program requires BOTH USMLE Step 1 and Step 2 to be completed at time of application, a notable step-gated filter that distinguishes it from many US observerships. Selected observers pay a $100 registration fee and complete health-insurance, criminal-background, drug-screen, HIPAA, and immunization requirements at least 60 days before arrival. The program explicitly disclaims that acceptance does not imply a residency or fellowship interview, and applicants may only be accepted once per 12-month period.
eligibilitySummary: International pediatric/FM trainees and physicians; USMLE Step 1 AND Step 2 required at application; one application per 12 months.

title: Griffin Hospital (Yale-affiliated) Clinical Observership
shortDescription: One-month observership at Yale-affiliated Griffin Hospital for ECFMG-certified IMGs with first-attempt USMLE passes.
fullDescription: Griffin Hospital in Derby, Connecticut, a Yale New Haven Health affiliate, runs a structured clinical observership focused on foreign medical graduates pursuing US training. The program is tightly gated: applicants must be graduates (not students), must already hold ECFMG certification, must have passed USMLE Step 1 on the first attempt, and must have scored at least 230 on Step 2 CK on the first attempt. Three observer slots are offered at a time across General Medicine, a Neuro-Cardiac Telemetry service, and a Flex Team. The rotation lasts four weeks and costs $900, payable six weeks before start; the fee is non-refundable. Applicants submit a completed application or ERAS common form, cover letter, CV, USMLE transcripts, ECFMG certificate, medical school diploma, at least one recent clinical reference, proof of health insurance, and a full immunization workup. English proficiency (TOEFL) is encouraged but not universally required. Griffin does not sponsor visas and does not provide housing. Observers bring their own white coat and clinical instruments. The program does not automatically produce LORs; observers must build relationships with preceptors and request them independently.
eligibilitySummary: ECFMG-certified IMG graduates only; USMLE Step 1 first-attempt pass, Step 2 CK ≥230 first attempt; valid visa and immunizations required.

title: USF Morsani International Medical Trainee (IMT) Observership
shortDescription: USF Morsani observership for final-year international students and IMG graduates across 22 specialties with staggered cohorts.
fullDescription: The International Medical Trainee (IMT) observership at USF Health Morsani College of Medicine is designed for final-year students and recent graduates of international medical schools not already affiliated with USF. Applicants in their final year must provide a medical-school approval letter and current transcript; graduates must have completed at least USMLE Step 1, and the Hospital Medicine track additionally requires Step 2 CK with a score of at least 240. The program offers rotations across more than twenty specialties including cardiology, emergency medicine, gastroenterology, neurology, ophthalmology, pulmonary medicine, and others, giving international candidates broad exposure to an academic US health system. Applications are filed through a Qualtrics portal in fixed staggered cycles (for example the Fall 2025 cycle opened April 1 and closed July 14); late submissions are rejected without exception. A separate clinical observership pathway exists but requires a pre-existing institutional agreement with the sending school. The public page does not specify fees, duration, certificate issuance, visa sponsorship, or LOR policies, so prospective applicants should confirm those details directly.

title: UHealth Clinical Observership Program — University of Miami
shortDescription: UHealth observership across 40+ specialties, up to 12 weeks, requiring a UHealth sponsor before applying.
fullDescription: The University of Miami UHealth Clinical Observership Program is a sponsor-gated shadowing experience for medical professionals seeking exposure to an academic South Florida health system. The core structural rule is that every applicant must secure a UHealth sponsor (a faculty member or credentialed clinician) and supply their full name and email in the application before the form will progress, which functions as a pre-match step. Rotations cannot exceed twelve weeks, and applications submitted fewer than three weeks before the intended start date are rejected. More than forty clinical areas are offered, spanning oncology, cardiology, pediatrics, surgical subspecialties, and others. The public application form collects citizenship, education, and work-history information and requires attestation to the program's rules, but does not surface details on fees, USMLE/ECFMG requirements, visa sponsorship, LORs, or certificates; a separate program handbook is referenced for those specifics. Applicants planning to use this rotation to strengthen a Match portfolio should clarify LOR and documentation practices with their sponsor before committing. UHealth is distinct from Jackson Health System, which operates its own separate observership track.

title: Baptist Health South Florida Visiting Physician Program
shortDescription: Baptist Health program for independently licensed practicing physicians; explicitly NOT for residency applicants or unlicensed IMGs.
fullDescription: The Visiting Physician Program at Baptist Health South Florida is aimed at independently licensed practicing physicians (MD, DO, DPM, DDS, DMD) who want to shadow Baptist clinicians in a peer-to-peer capacity. Baptist makes the scope explicit: the program is not for residency applicants, not for students seeking academic credit, and not for IMGs who do not yet hold US licensure. Rotations range from a single day up to four weeks at the sponsoring physician's discretion. Applicants must first identify a Baptist physician or employee sponsor, then submit a government-issued ID, current medical license copy, signed Sponsor Approval and Supplemental Health Form, and an online application. A non-refundable $450 administrative fee is required. Applications must be filed at least 15 business days before the intended start date. International applicants go through an additional eligibility review. Because the program is gated on US licensure and explicitly excludes US-match pathways, it sits in the 'specialty visiting' category.

title: Mount Sinai Miami International Postgraduate Observership
shortDescription: Four-week Mount Sinai Miami observership with hard USMLE ≥220 floor on every completed Step and a certificate of attendance.
fullDescription: Mount Sinai Medical Center in Miami Beach runs an International Postgraduate Observership organized as monthly four-week cohorts starting on the first Monday of every month year-round. The program is written for international physicians who want a compressed, structured month inside a US academic-affiliated hospital. Applications are due at least 90 days before the intended start date, and the center reviews submissions within roughly six weeks. Mount Sinai is explicit about a hard USMLE floor: applicants must score at least 220 on every Step they have completed (Step 1, Step 2, and Step 3 if taken), with no exceptions. Sufficient English proficiency is also required. A registration fee is collected only upon acceptance, and no refunds are issued for cancellations. On completion, observers are issued a certificate of attendance, a meaningful differentiator from observerships offering only an attendance record.

Pediatric Neurosurgery Clinical Observership Program
The Clinical Observership Program provides domestic and international observers the ability to follow our highly skilled neurosurgeons for up to 60 days. This program is a 'no hands on' patient contact and must be with the Sponsoring Medical Staff Member throughout the duration of the Observership program. During this program, observers will be able to:
Witness some of the most complex neurosurgical fetal and pediatric cases in our operating rooms.
Attend patient care and evaluation in an outpatient setting.
Engage in daily patient rounds where treatment of care is determined for patients with multiple levels of acuity.
Exposure to a multidisciplinary approach to complex cases with numerous neuroscience specialties.
Eligible candidates include neurosurgery physicians, fellows, residents, medical students, and college students interested in neurosurgery.

The documentation below is required for approval of preferred duration of Observership. All documents and fees should be collected by Medical Staff Services 3 months prior to preferred start date. After Medical Staff Services receives all needed documentation, it will take approximately 30 days to process for an approval. Those documents include:
Evidence of a TB test within the last year
Acceptable TB Tests: PPD skin test, QuantiFERON-TB Gold Test, and chest x-rays
Preferred time frame of Observership (60 consecutive days max)
Brief synopsis of why you want to complete this Observership
CV or Resume
Two Letters of Recommendation from Medical School Dean's Office or Neurosurgery Residency Program Director

title: Emory University SOM Visiting Student / Clinical Observership
shortDescription: Emory fourth-year visiting student electives with dedicated international track, TOEFL requirement, and October-February placement window.
fullDescription: Emory University School of Medicine operates a formal visiting student electives program that splits into two distinct pathways: one for fourth-year US MD and DO students from AAMC-member medical schools, and a second for fourth-year international medical students from non-US schools. The US track applies through AAMC's VSLO platform and pays $100 application + $260 registration. The international track applies on Emory's own timeline — affiliated schools April 15 to May 15, 2026; non-affiliated schools May 16 to June 30, 2026 — and pays $525 application + $4,500 tuition. International applicants must submit TOEFL iBT with a minimum composite of 20 and no sub-score below 5, official transcript, USMLE Step 1 report, and a dean's letter confirming good standing, final-year status, and completion of five core clerkships. Because this is an elective rather than a pure shadowing observership, participants are embedded in clinical teams in a student role. International students are placed only during October-February, effectively aligning with US fourth-year residency application season.
cost: US: $100 app + $260 reg. Intl: $525 app + $4,500 tuition.
eligibilitySummary: Fourth-year MD/DO (US track) or fourth-year international medical students; TOEFL iBT (min 20) and USMLE Step 1 required for international track.

title: Grady Health System Medical Education Observership
shortDescription: Grady Health observership across multiple career stages with tiered pricing and a sponsor-first application in ACEMAPP.
fullDescription: Grady Health System in Atlanta runs a broad medical education observership that accepts an unusually wide applicant pool: high school students 16+, domestic pre-clinical undergraduates and graduate students, international medical students, international medical graduates not in an ACGME program, and international practicing physicians. Standard observerships run roughly thirty days, and extensions up to ninety days are possible. Fees are tiered by applicant type: $25/day for single-day observerships, $100 for high school students, $250 for domestic pre-clinical/grad students, and $1,000 for international medical students, IMG graduates, or international physicians. Applicants must first identify a Medical Staff physician or Advanced Practice Provider sponsor; the inquiry email to medicaleducation@gmh.edu must include both applicant and sponsor details. After that, applicants complete the Grady ACEMAPP application, fulfill required training modules, sign confidentiality and liability agreements, clear health screening, and submit valid ID, CV, and professional photo. Applications close between June 1 and August 31, 2026 during an annual blackout.

title: Indiana University SOM Global Outreach Pathology Observership
shortDescription: Four-week IU pathology observership with sub-specialty rotations and LOR access from supervising attendings.
fullDescription: Indiana University School of Medicine runs a four-week pathology observership for international and US physicians who want direct exposure to an academic diagnostic lab and its subspecialty services. The program is administered by the Department of Pathology and Laboratory Medicine under Program Director Muhammad Idrees, MD, and applicants submit through a single Qualtrics portal open annually from January 1 to March 1, with acceptance decisions released in April. Applicants must provide an updated medical CV, a medical school transcript (translated into English if needed), both USMLE Step 1 and Step 2 scores, and a headshot. Incomplete applications are not reviewed. Once accepted, observers have seven days to sign and return the invitation letter, and schedules arrive one week before start. During the four weeks, observers participate in clinical and didactic laboratory experiences and may request specific subspecialties. The department is explicit that observers may request letters of recommendation from attendings with whom they have worked, which is especially useful for IMGs preparing a residency application. There is no application fee and no stipend; observers are responsible for their own lodging, travel, meals, and visa logistics.

auditData.title: Indiana University SOM Global Outreach Pathology Observership  [IN · Indianapolis] (FIX URL)

page_excerpts.text: The Global Outreach Observership Program at the IU School of Medicine Department of Pathology and Laboratory Medicine provides qualified medical graduates the opportunity to learn more about our practicing pathologists and their purpose within the greater health care system through a robust interaction with anatomical and clinical pathology. The observership program is intended for national and international individuals seeking further pathology training which may lead to a residency position in the U.S. in the field of pathology.

An observership in our laboratory exposes physicians to four full weeks of clinical and didactic experiences. Observers must be responsible for their own lodging, travel, meals, visas, etc. While we do not charge for an observership, we also do not offer stipends.
Observers can expect to be in the laboratory Monday through Friday between the hours of 7:30 a.m. and 4:30 p.m. ET.

Pathology-focused activities during an observership rotation occur at the Indiana University Health Pathology Laboratory located at 350 W. 11th Street, Indianapolis, IN, 46202. Our affiliate facilities include but are not limited to IU Health University Hospital, IU Health Methodist Hospital, Marion County Coroner's Office, and IU School of Medicine on the campus of IU Indianapolis.

The observership program provides no compensation, stipend or reimbursement. All arrangements and expenses, including but not limited to travel, lodging, travel documents, immunizations, health insurance and living costs will be the full responsibility of the observer.

Unless scheduled to work with the program director on a sub-specialty, observers are not encouraged to ask for a letter of recommendation from the program director but are encouraged to interact with the attending pathologists with whom they spend quality time.

Applicants must submit the following documents to be considered for the program. Applications lacking any of the listed materials are not considered. Applicants will pick one subspecialty to complete their observations in.
Updated medical curriculum vitae (CV)
Medical school transcripts (translated if non-English)
USMLE Step 1 score
USMLE Step 2 score
Headshot

Be proficient in the English language.
Be financially able to incur all costs associated with the program, including but not limited to travel, lodging, insurance and living expenses.
Have all applicable visas procured by the start of the rotation.
Have all required immunizations prior to the start of the rotation.
Agree to review and adhere to the policies, regulations and practices of IU School of Medicine and IU Health.
Breast · GU · GI · Head and Neck · GYN Renal · Heart and Lung · Neuropathology · Bone and Soft Tissue · Dermatopathology · Cytology · Microbiology · Blood Bank · Chemistry · Hemepath · Molecular

Muhammad Idrees, MD — Global Outreach Observership Program Director — Louis Y. Mazzini Professor of Pathology
Julie Connor — julconno@iu.edu (do not send application materials through email; all applications via application form)

title: UofL Health Medical Observership Program (MOP)
shortDescription: Licensed-physician observership at UofL Health with year-round intake and 60-day lead time.
fullDescription: UofL Health runs the Medical Observership Program (MOP) for physicians who have already completed medical school and are currently licensed. International applicants must pass an English test: TOEFL at 79 internet-based or 550 paper-based, or OET at 300 written and 350 for listening, reading, and speaking. Placements are first-come, first-served and can reach up to four weeks per specialty across clinic, acute care, and procedural areas. Applications are accepted year-round through a SurveyMonkey form, but must be submitted at least 60 days before the target start date. The $250 application fee is charged once the applicant is invited to proceed, and observers must supply MMR, Tdap, Hepatitis B, and varicella records, a TB test within the last 12 months, an influenza record Nov-Mar, a CV, and a physician letter of recommendation. The program is strictly observational: no unsupervised patient interaction, no direct physical contact, and no EMR access.
eligibilitySummary: Licensed physicians only; international applicants must pass TOEFL or OET; 60-day lead time.

title: Ochsner Health International Observership Program
shortDescription: Faculty-sponsored international observership at Ochsner, up to 90 days, with non-refundable processing fee.
fullDescription: Ochsner Health's Office of International Academics runs an international observership specifically for physicians who already have a faculty sponsor inside Ochsner. The program does not accept cold outreach: the Office only evaluates applicants who have been referred by an Ochsner faculty provider, so a sponsor is effectively the entry requirement. Applicants must fall into one of three defined categories: active trainees in a foreign clinical program, employed physicians who care for critically ill patients, or those with institutional or government sponsorship. English fluency is mandatory. The maximum duration is 90 days and the program does not grant extensions. A $500 non-refundable processing fee is required, and the program has to be applied for at least 90 days before the desired start date. Inside the rotation, participants cannot do hands-on patient care, write in charts, answer patient questions, or receive board clinical credit, but they can attend rounds, observe clinics and labs, participate in conferences, and use the medical library.
eligibilitySummary: International physicians in one of three categories and referred by an Ochsner faculty sponsor; cold applications rejected.

title: Brigham & Women's Global Emergency Medicine & Critical Care Training
shortDescription: Year-long BWH observership + research training across ICUs and ED for international clinician-scientists.
fullDescription: The Brigham and Women's Hospital Global Emergency Medicine and Critical Care Training Program is a long-form observership-plus-research experience designed for international physicians who want to train as clinician-scientists. Under Program Director Peter Hou, MD, and Division Chief Raghu Seethala, MD, the default appointment runs twelve months and BWH does not consider requests shorter than six months. Trainees rotate two months each through Surgical, Thoracic, Cardiovascular, Neurological, and Medical ICUs and then two months in the Emergency Department, all as observers without direct patient care responsibilities. Alongside the clinical rotations, each trainee develops a research project, learns study design and methodology, and must complete at least one publishable work. Curriculum deliverables include a hospital overview paper, a quality-improvement project, and a final end-of-program presentation. Tuition is $5,000/month with an advance deposit; eligibility screening is routed through the Mass General Brigham Office for Global Professionals and Scholars (GPS).
eligibilitySummary: International physicians pursuing clinician-scientist careers; minimum 6-month commitment; screened by Mass General Brigham GPS.
stepRequirements: USMLE or COMLEX Step 1 and Step 2 required

auditData.title: BIDMC Interventional Radiology Visiting Observership  [MA · Boston]

page_excerpts.text:
The Interventional Radiology (IR) section at Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center proudly offers an International Visiting Observership Program. Our mission for this program is to immerse aspiring and seasoned global IRs in BIDMC Radiology's top-tier IR inpatient and ambulatory service. Through a tailored learning experience, we aim to:
Introduce participants to BIDMC IR-specific areas of expertise.
Expose participants to daily morning rounds, case discussions, and real-time observation.
Provide structured learning opportunities (e.g., didactic lectures and presentations).
Create a two-way avenue for expertise exchange.

Interventional Radiology: Practicing community and academic radiologists, residents, fellows and medical students with an interest in image-guided interventions.
Other specialties: Clinical physicians in the surgical and medical disciplines of oncology, GI, transplant, urology and acute care surgery.

The rotation is designed to immerse observers in the BIDMC-IR experience through attending morning rounds, case discussions, and real-time observation of cases. It is not designed to support pre-residency endeavors for aspiring applicants. The program cannot support research efforts or issue letters in support of medical residency applications.
International observers will obtain their own tourist Visa. A J1 Visa sponsorship is not offered from the BIDMC for this course of study.

Tuition is $2,000.00/month. Tuition is payable on confirmation of acceptance.
Please note: This fee is non-refundable as we have a commitment to not overbook

Observerships are offered during the academic year for one- to six-month periods. Along with your completed application, please include your CV, a personal statement, and three letters of recommendation.
Please email your completed application to Katelyn Sullivan, Radiology Education Manager.

The program is available to high school, undergraduate and medical students, as well as residents and practicing physicians.

This is a hands-off experience with no benefits, salary, stipends, medical insurance, transportation, accommodations or academic credit. Program participants will observe clinic, operating room and departmental functions. They may not contact patients or be responsible for their care.
Limited to ages 18 and above (age 16 or 17 with parental permission)
No more than two weeks duration
No access to patient rooms on isolation precautions

Current SLU undergraduate or medical students who have questions or wish to schedule shadowing opportunities should contact Tina Vandever at ent@slucare.ssmhealth.com or call 314-617-3958.
Medical school graduates must apply through the graduate medical education office. Please allow up to 90 days for processing. For more information, email gme@slucare.ssmhealth.com.

title: UNMC International Neurology Observership
shortDescription: One-month UNMC neurology observership for international physicians; $1,500 fee. Rotation slots filled through Jan 2026.
fullDescription: The University of Nebraska Medical Center Department of Neurological Sciences runs a one-month international observership aimed at physicians with a clear commitment to a career in neurology. The application path is staged: interested physicians review the overview document, submit the online application, and if approved send full documentation before paying the $1,500 program fee within two weeks of invoice. If the applicant cancels before the rotation starts, a $200 administrative fee is deducted; there are no refunds once the rotation begins. Participants are not involved in direct patient care but do join discussions, patient-oriented conferences, and didactic sessions. UNMC is explicit that participants arrange their own visa, transportation, travel medical insurance, and housing. The page currently states that all rotation slots have been filled through January 2026.

The operational paperwork is unusually granular including Documents A, B, C, F, and H, a guidelines packet, workflow document, checklist, correspondence templates, HIPAA training and exam, immunization documentation, and auxiliary services support. Duration, costs, USMLE and ECFMG requirements, visa sponsorship, LOR policy, and certificate issuance are not detailed on the program page.

title: CHOP International Observership Program

fullDescription: The Children's Hospital of Philadelphia International Observership Program, run by Global Pediatric Education, is a two- to eight-week pediatric observership aimed at internationally based clinicians, and it is explicit about who it excludes. Only three applicant types are accepted: a fully trained healthcare professional currently working abroad and returning abroad, a medical fellow or resident enrolled in a foreign program and returning there, or a health-field student without a terminal degree who intends to practice outside the US. CHOP explicitly excludes medical students seeking clinical credit and, critically for any USMLE-IMG audience, anyone using the observership to pursue US graduate medical training. English fluency is mandatory. The program charges a single $750 administrative fee plus a background screening fee that varies by country. CHOP is unusually explicit on two IMG-relevant policies: faculty do not provide LORs because observers do not perform hands-on clinical work, and participants do receive a certificate documenting the dates of the observership.

title: Penn Radiology Visiting Observers Program
shortDescription: Division-by-division radiology observerships across Penn Medicine's imaging sections for international trainees and practicing radiologists.
fullDescription: Penn Radiology runs an umbrella Visiting Observers Program that lets international medical students, foreign-trained radiologists, and post-graduate physicians shadow inside individual subspecialty sections at the Hospital of the University of Pennsylvania. The program frames itself as an enrichment experience for the visitor — observers cannot replace departmental staffing, cannot touch patients, and are not awarded academic credit. The participating divisions include Abdominal Imaging, Breast Imaging, Cardiovascular Imaging, Thoracic Imaging, Musculoskeletal, Neuroradiology, and Nuclear Medicine, and each keeps its own coordinator who decides whether to accept a given applicant. Applications go directly to the division of interest using the Penn Visiting Observership Application. Immigration status is handled by Penn Medicine itself: approved observers enter on a J-1 exchange visitor visa (processing can take six to nine months) or, for stays under three months, a B-1 business visitor visa. Before the rotation begins, observers must document medical coverage of at least $50,000 per incident, current MMR and varicella immunizations, a negative PPD within three months, and — for September through March arrivals — a flu shot.

cost: Not published; applicants must carry ≥$50,000 medical coverage

title: Penn Dermatopathology International Observership
shortDescription: Year-long dermatopathology observership at Penn for physicians who have already completed dermatology or pathology residency.
fullDescription: Penn Dermatology runs a single highly structured dermatopathology observership slot each academic year aimed at physicians who are already residency-trained in either dermatology or pathology and want a year of immersion in a US academic dermatopathology service. The twelve-month format mirrors a fellowship calendar, running July through June, and the observer spends their time at multiheaded microscopes with faculty, participating in sign-out of biopsies, attending teaching conferences, and working through the department's case archives. Because the program only accepts one observer per cycle and requires English fluency, selection is competitive and driven by CV strength, research interest, and faculty fit with Program Director Emily Chu, MD, PhD. Applicants must submit a full package including three LORs, personal statement, CV, notarized English-translated medical degree, transcripts, and explicit evidence of how tuition will be paid. Tuition is $10,000 for the year, with half due on day one and the remainder by the six-month mark. The program runs on J-1 exchange visitor sponsorship. April 1, 2026 deadline is for a July 2027 start.
eligibilitySummary: Must have completed residency in Dermatology or Pathology, financial sponsorship, J-1 visa eligibility, English proficiency.

title: Thomas Jefferson University Clinical Observerships
shortDescription: Jefferson's centralized short-term clinical observer pathway for international students, IMGs, and visiting healthcare professionals.
fullDescription: Thomas Jefferson University centralizes short-term clinical observation through its Office of International Services (OIS) rather than running a single program. In practice, this means an individual clinical department at Jefferson has to agree to host the visitor first, and only then does OIS process the sponsorship request through its International Services Portal. The setup accommodates three main groups: international students from any academic discipline who want clinical exposure (capped at twelve weeks), international medical graduates looking for US observer time, and practicing physicians or other healthcare professionals. Total observer time at Jefferson is capped at ninety days. The university notes that it can take up to three months to clear Occupational Health, HR, immunization, and badge requirements before the rotation begins. Once cleared, the observer completes an orientation and then reports to the hosting department. Information on tuition, letters of recommendation, certificates, USMLE, or ECFMG is not published; prospective applicants should assume those items are set at the departmental level.
duration: Up to 90 days; onboarding can take up to 3 months
cost: Not published; observer arranges housing and insurance
eligibilitySummary: International students, IMGs, and licensed healthcare professionals; must be hosted by a Jefferson department and cleared through OIS.

title: Trinity Health St. Mary Clinical Observership Program
shortDescription: IMG-focused internal medicine observership at Trinity Health's St. Mary Medical Center (Langhorne, PA).
fullDescription: St. Mary Medical Center's Clinical Observership Program is explicitly aimed at international medical graduates who want structured internal medicine exposure inside a US teaching hospital. Observers attend the full educational fabric of the department — noon lectures, case conferences, workshops, and grand rounds — and shadow inpatient teams and clinic attendings with weekly structured feedback from the precepting physician. The program is strict about what observers cannot do: no physical examinations, no history taking, no procedures, and no EMR access. Research involvement is also off limits. This framing matters because it lines up with what US residency programs expect an IMG observer rotation to look like, which is one of the reasons St. Mary's program is valued as a Match-prep experience. The program requires a short white lab coat, enforces a professional dress code, and mandates health insurance with evacuation and repatriation benefits. Housing and transportation are on the candidate, though parking is free. St. Mary sits inside Trinity Health Mid-Atlantic's GME structure, so the observership is naturally oriented toward IMGs applying to US IM residencies.
cost: Not published; observer pays for housing, transport, and insurance
eligibilitySummary: International medical graduates; must cover own housing/transport and carry insurance with evacuation and repatriation coverage.
stepRequirements: USMLE Step 1 AND Step 2 both required; ECFMG certificate or scores due 4 weeks before start

title: Texas Tech HSC Internal Medicine IMG Observership
shortDescription: Structured, paid internal medicine observership at Texas Tech aimed at USMLE-cleared IMGs.
fullDescription: Texas Tech's Internal Medicine observership is one of the more transparent IMG-oriented programs in the country, with the costs, timelines, and eligibility criteria all published up front. Applicants must have graduated from an ECFMG-approved medical school, be at least sixteen years old, and have already completed USMLE Step 1 and Step 2 — both score reports are required as part of the application. Those whose countries require US visas must also provide valid visa documentation to TTUHSC before onboarding. The program operates in four-week or eight-week blocks; more than two experiences exceeding eight weeks require special approval. Placement is first-come, first-served through a dedicated portal. The fee schedule is $250 for a non-refundable application, roughly $100 for a criminal background check, and then either $2,000 for a four-week rotation or $3,800 for an eight-week rotation. Applicants upload a CV, headshot, letter of recommendation, medical school transcript, COVID vaccination card, and USMLE score reports at submission. Clinically, the rotation is a hands-off shadowing experience. In addition to general internal medicine, rotations can be arranged in nephrology, infectious diseases, and pulmonary/critical care.
eligibilitySummary: ECFMG-approved medical school graduate; USMLE Step 1 AND Step 2 required; valid visa if applicable; LOR at application.
stepRequirements: USMLE Step 1 AND Step 2 both required; ECFMG certificate or scores due 4 weeks before start

title: MD Anderson Cancer Center Observer Program
shortDescription: Observation-only outreach program at MD Anderson — no visa sponsorship, no LORs issued.
fullDescription: MD Anderson's Observer Programs give visiting physicians, trainees, and students hands-in-pocket exposure to one of the largest cancer centers in the world, but the program's guardrails are unusually strict. Participants must be at least eighteen, employed or enrolled at an academic or healthcare institution, have written approval from that home institution, demonstrate English proficiency, be entirely self-funded, and already hold a valid visa — MD Anderson is explicit that it does not sponsor visas. Two tracks are offered: the main Observer track runs six to ninety consecutive days per calendar year; the shorter STEP Visitor track allows one-to-five-day visits up to every six months. Professionals pay $2,000 total, while trainees and students are not charged. The application flow begins with securing a faculty sponsor. Two features stand out: MD Anderson is explicit that certificates of completion and letters of recommendation will not be issued; verification letters are available on request. Clinical activity is strictly observational.

title: UT Southwestern Plastic Surgery Observership
shortDescription: Short unpaid plastic surgery observership for MD-level international physicians, Nov–May only.
fullDescription: UT Southwestern's Plastic Surgery observership is open exclusively to physicians holding an MD or equivalent (PhDs are explicitly ineligible), and it runs only during the November 1 through May 31 window of each academic year, with the summer closed to observers. Rotations are capped at six weeks unless the observer secures written approval to extend. The experience is strictly observational — the observer cannot participate in clinical decision making and cannot do hands-on work — and the program repeats that the rotation does not constitute medical education and therefore does not confer academic credit. The International Affairs office assists with immigration, typically via B-1 Visitor for Business visa or J-1 Research Scholar visa. Candidates must submit a completed Observer Application Form, MD degree copy, letter from home institution confirming good standing, CV, English-language immunization records, documentation of at least $2,920 USD in funding, and health insurance with repatriation.

title: UVA Breast Imaging International Visiting Scholars
shortDescription: Tuition-free two-month UVA breast imaging mini-fellowship for international radiologists returning to practice abroad.
fullDescription: UVA Radiology runs a structured Breast Imaging International Visiting Scholars Program for physicians who already hold an MD, DO, or equivalent degree and have completed a diagnostic radiology residency. Scholars are selected with the intent that they will return to practice in their home countries, and UVA screens for that in selection. Each scholar spends two months embedded in the breast imaging service, rotating alongside faculty for diagnostic readouts, procedures, and multidisciplinary case reviews. UVA runs three cohorts per year, starting in August, October, and December. Although the program is tuition-free, scholars remain responsible for housing, travel, and living costs. Applications go to program coordinator Nicole Vaughan and require desired fellowship dates, cover letter, CV, three LORs, and medical school transcript. A virtual interview rounds out the competitive selection.

auditData.title: UVA Breast Imaging International Visiting Scholars

page_excerpts.text:
Program Description
The purpose of this scholar program is to improve breast imaging knowledge and practice at foreign medical institutions. Candidates must be employed by and planning to return to an international institution. There is no tuition required for this program.

Visiting scholars will be in residence for a 2-month period in the Breast Imaging Division of the UVA Breast Care Center. Clinical observation will include all aspects of breast imaging—screening and diagnostic mammography, tomosynthesis, contrast-enhanced mammography... biopsy, cyst aspiration, wire localization, radioactive seed localization, and galactography. International Visiting Scholars are expected to be present during business hours and be actively engaged in the learning process.
Note: Program participants will not be granted clinical privileges or have a medical license, therefore they will have no hands-on patient contact.

Must be an M.D., D.O., or other physician degree equivalent.
Must have completed a residency program in diagnostic radiology (U.S. or international)
Must be planning to return to practice in home country
Must be proficient in spoken and written English language
This is a non-salaried observership position. The scholar must provide evidence of financial support during the visiting scholar period; typically, salary is provided by the home institution or national government

title: Panamerican Trauma / VCU International Observership
shortDescription: Short-duration international trauma observership coordinated by Panamerican Trauma Society with VCU.
fullDescription: The Panamerican Trauma Society's International Observership, organized in partnership with Virginia Commonwealth University's International Trauma System Development Program, gives international medical students, physicians, and fellows structured exposure to the American trauma ecosystem. Rotations span the full continuum: pre-hospital care and EMS systems, the trauma bay, the acute care ICU, post-hospital rehabilitation, the simulation center, performance improvement initiatives, and trauma ultrasound. Each observership is strictly shadow-only — the program explicitly prohibits patient care, examinations, procedures, or research. Observers receive a certificate of attendance. Duration ranges from two weeks to roughly one month. All rotations begin on the first Monday of the month, so applicants must apply at least three months in advance. Applicants must be medical school graduates, demonstrate English proficiency, and secure their own B-1 visa.
duration: Two weeks to one month; first Monday starts
cost: App processing fee (amount not disclosed); covers own travel/living
eligibilitySummary: Medical school graduates (students also welcomed); English proficient; B-1 visa and expenses self-covered.
stepRequirements: USMLE Step 1 required; score submitted with application

page_excerpts.text:
INTERNATIONAL OBSERVERSHIP PROGRAM
The ITSDP International Observership Program (IOP) allows program participants to observe different components of trauma and emergency systems at VCU Department of Surgery, Division of Acute Care Surgical Services, Richmond Ambulance Authority, and the VCU Center for Trauma and Critical Care Education.
The goal of the IOP is to facilitate knowledge transfer from VCU Health System experts to foreign medical professionals through an informal shadowing program.

The IOP offers international medical students, physicians, and fellows the opportunity to observe our surgeons, physicians, and residents in training and participate in discussions, patient-oriented activities, and academic sessions.

Observers will be able to observe their host faculty in the clinical setting and attend lectures and meetings.
Observers will be accompanied by an attending physician, fellow, resident, healthcare staff, or supervisor at all times when observing patients.
Observers will not be able to perform or be involved in any patient care or research.
Observers will not have access to patient records, library, or email privileges.
The duration of the observership varies from 2 weeks up to 1 months upon the observers' academic level and objectives. The IOP starts every first Monday of the month...

title: UW DLMP Global Observership (Pathology)
shortDescription: One-month UW pathology observership with stipend, requiring USMLE Step 1 and a required LOR.
fullDescription: UW's Department of Laboratory Medicine and Pathology runs a one-month Global Observership Program aimed at medical school graduates who are not currently enrolled in a residency program and who want formal US pathology exposure before applying for training. The program stands out for two reasons: it requires applicants to have already passed USMLE Step 1 and submit the score, and it offers a stipend of up to $2,500 to offset housing, food, and travel costs. Applications run through Embark and must include a CV, USMLE Step 1 score, two-page personal statement, short-answer response, and contact information for a recommender who will submit a letter of recommendation — making the LOR a structural part of the program. Visa handling is the applicant's responsibility; UW accepts B1/B2, F1, or permanent-resident status. The observer shadows faculty, residents, and fellows across surgical pathology, clinical pathology, and select subspecialty rotations. Demonstrated prior experience or clear interest in pathology is preferred.
cost: No fee; program provides stipend up to $2,500
eligibilitySummary: Medical school graduates not currently in residency; USMLE Step 1 passed; pathology interest; must hold own visa.

title: UW Madison Pathology Observership
shortDescription: Four-week summer pathology observership for external medical school graduates at UW Madison.
fullDescription: UW Madison's Department of Pathology and Laboratory Medicine offers a four-week in-person observership in summer, with two fixed 2026 blocks: May 26 through June 19, and July 6 through 31. The program is open only to external medical school graduates — meaning not for current residents elsewhere — and targets physicians preparing for a career in pathology. Observers rotate through surgical pathology, autopsy service, transfusion medicine, and the clinical pathology core, sitting with attendings and residents during sign-out. The program is explicit that it does not offer a remote or virtual observership. Applications are submitted through a Google Form and must include a CV; if USMLE Step scores are available, the department asks applicants to include them, but scores are not strictly mandatory. The deadline is March 1 of the intended academic year, and notifications are issued by March 20.

title: UW Madison International Observership in Urology
shortDescription: Selective urology observership at UW Madison for international physicians with prior urology experience.
fullDescription: UW Madison's Department of Urology runs a selective International Observership designed for physicians from outside the United States who already have prior urology experience. Observers are integrated into the department's clinical and educational activities — they observe patient care, attend conferences, and participate in didactic sessions — but they are strictly prohibited from direct patient care and cannot access the EMR. The program is explicit that enrollment is tightly limited and that the selection process weighs both availability of faculty hosts and the depth of the applicant's existing urology experience. Applicants should expect an email notification about application status within six weeks. The main public page does not specify duration, cost, USMLE or ECFMG requirements, visa sponsorship, LOR expectations, or certificate issuance; those details are housed under separate linked sections.
eligibilitySummary: International physicians with prior urology experience; selection based on availability and prior specialty experience.

auditData.title: UW Madison International Observership in Urology

page_excerpts.text:
International Observership in Urology
The University of Wisconsin Department of Urology offers an International Observership Program focused in Urology for select physicians [to] enhance knowledge in general urology, in urology subspecialties, advances in urology, and to provide exposure to urological care as practiced in one of the foremost training centers in the United States.

This program will offer participating physicians intense observer activity in the field of urology. Participants will observe patient care as it is rendered by their supervising physician(s) who may also work with fellows and housestaff. Participants are not permitted to have any direct patient care or access the medical record, and will have the status of Observers. Observers will also participate in discussions, patient-oriented conferences, and other didactic sessions.

Registration is strictly limited to assure a meaningful experience for those enrolled in this program. Selected on the basis of availability and previous experience in urology, applicants will be notified by email of the status of their application within six weeks of receipt of their complete application.

title: Johns Hopkins Visiting Medical Student Clerkship
shortDescription: Four-week clinical and 4-9 week research electives at one of the top US academic medical centers via Hopkins' own Slate portal (not VSLO).
fullDescription: Johns Hopkins School of Medicine hosts visiting 4th-year medical students for four-week clinical electives and 4-9 week research electives across its departments at The Johns Hopkins Hospital and affiliated sites. Applications are submitted through Hopkins' Slate portal (not the AAMC VSLO platform) and applicants can file up to three applications per cycle. Visiting student placements occur after JHUSOM students are scheduled, so priority is space-dependent and decisions take 30-60 days. The research elective fee is rising from $500 to $750 for rotations starting Fall 2026. Applicants are expected to have completed core clerkships and to be enrolled at a medical school with an executed affiliation agreement with Hopkins. The clinical elective portfolio covers medicine, surgery, pediatrics, OB/GYN, psychiatry, neurology, and many subspecialties. The program is demanding and residency-adjacent: departments treat the rotation as both an academic experience and an unofficial audition.
cost: $500 research elective fee ($750 starting Fall 2026); clinical tuition varies by school agreement
eligibilitySummary: 4th-year medical students from LCME- or COCA-accredited schools with home-school affiliation agreement; core clerkships completed; good standing.
stepRequirements: USMLE Step 1 or COMLEX Level 1 typically required
adminNotes:
Verified 2026-04-17. Hopkins uses Slate, not VSLO.

title: Yale Visiting International Student Elective Program
shortDescription: Four-week clinical electives at Yale for final-year international medical students via AAMC VSLO. Hard deadline four months before start.
fullDescription: The Yale Visiting International Student Elective Program is designed for final-year medical students enrolled at international (non-US) medical schools. Students apply through AAMC VSLO and may complete up to three four-week electives, each in a different specialty. Electives are strictly elective-level (no core clerkships and no observerships). Requirements include a dean's letter verifying good standing and core-clerkship completion, English proficiency (TOEFL/IELTS for non-native speakers), official transcript, CV, recommendation letter, immunization records, personal statement, and proof of health insurance upon acceptance. Yale states that clinical electives do not satisfy home-school core clerkship requirements. The 2026-2027 rate is $4,775 for 4 weeks, $9,550 for 8, $14,325 for 12. Applications open each February for the first semester and September for the second semester, and must be submitted no later than four months before the chosen start. Estimated monthly living costs (excluding housing) are at least $2,240.
duration: 4 weeks per elective; up to 3 electives (12 weeks total)
eligibilitySummary: Final-year international medical students in good standing with all core clerkships completed; English proficient; VSLO-enabled home school.

title: Harvard Medical School Visiting Students Program
specialty: 200+ clerkships across specialties at HMS-affiliated hospitals
shortDescription: Over 200 clerkship options at HMS affiliates including MGH, BWH, Boston Children's, Dana-Farber, and McLean, applied via AAMC VSLO.
fullDescription: The HMS Visiting Students Program accepts final-year medical students into 4-week clerkship blocks at Harvard's ten affiliated teaching hospitals, including Massachusetts General Hospital, Brigham and Women's Hospital, Boston Children's Hospital, Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, and McLean Hospital. With more than 200 clerkship listings and combined programs that rotate students across multiple affiliates during a single block, the program is unusually broad in specialty coverage. Research opportunities and observerships are explicitly not offered through this pathway. Applications are processed by the HMS Registrar's Office and must be submitted through AAMC VSLO; rotation dates are fixed and must match the HMS calendar. Separate pathways exist for equity-focused applicants (Visiting Clerkship Program via the Office for Culture and Community Engagement) and for emergency medicine (MGB EM Clerkship). Applicants should assume competitive selection: HMS receives thousands of applications per year.
cost: Tuition published under Scheduling & Tuition; VSLO platform fees apply

title: Mass General Brigham Emergency Medicine Clerkship (HMS)
shortDescription: Combined EM rotation at Brigham and Women's + MGH for 4th-year US medical students via HMS VSLO.
fullDescription: The MGB EM Clerkship is a combined emergency medicine rotation that places visiting 4th-year medical students in clinical shifts at both Brigham and Women's Hospital and Massachusetts General Hospital — two of Harvard's highest-volume emergency departments. Co-directed by faculty at each site, it is one of the most sought-after EM aways on the East Coast for students applying into emergency medicine residency. Applications are processed via AAMC VSLO through the HMS Registrar; materials are due at least three months before the desired start date and reviews begin in mid-March for summer and fall placements. Rotation dates run January through October, aligning with Harvard's block schedule. Currently the program accepts students only from US medical schools. Visiting students can access housing guidance through the HMS Housing and Transportation page. The program is widely regarded as a residency audition for Harvard EM.
cost: No separate tuition for US-enrolled students; HMS registrar fees apply

title: Harvard Visiting Clerkship Scholars Program (VCSP)
shortDescription: Immersive 4-week HMS clerkship layering mentorship, leadership development, and community engagement on top of a standard clerkship.
fullDescription: The Visiting Clerkship Scholars Program (VCSP), run by the HMS Office for Culture and Community Engagement, layers structured mentorship, leadership development, and community engagement on top of a standard HMS visiting clerkship. It is aimed at fourth-year and qualified third-year students from groups underrepresented in medicine or from disadvantaged backgrounds who have demonstrated service leadership. Participants rotate at a Harvard-affiliated teaching hospital and join a cohort for additional programming. The companion Visiting Clerkship Program (VCP) provides similar support through the HMS Exchange Clerkship Program. Applicants complete a supplemental application in addition to the standard HMS VSLO visiting student application. Selection is competitive and cohort-based. VCSP is one of the most well-known equity-focused visiting clerkship pipelines in the country.
cost: Supplemental stipend/support; standard HMS VSLO fees still apply
eligibilitySummary: 4th-year or qualified 3rd-year US medical students from URiM or disadvantaged backgrounds.
stepRequirements: Step 1 or COMLEX Level 1 strongly encouraged

fullDescription: Weill Cornell Medicine's Office of International Medical Student Education runs a dedicated visiting-student pathway for final-year medical students enrolled at schools outside the US and Canada. All applications must be coordinated through the AAMC VSLO platform — direct contact with clinical departments is grounds for disqualification. Electives are available in Medicine, Emergency Medicine, Obstetrics and Gynecology, Pediatrics, Dermatology, Anesthesiology, and more; maximum elective time is two months. title: Cleveland Clinic Elective Program
shortDescription: Four-week clinical electives at Cleveland Clinic main + Florida campuses via VSLO. Requires USMLE Step 1 + 10-panel drug test (as of Jan 2026).
fullDescription: The Cleveland Clinic Elective Program is a clinical elective track for final-year medical students run in partnership with the Cleveland Clinic Lerner College of Medicine of CWRU. Students apply through AAMC VSLO for four-week rotations at the main Cleveland campus, with some offerings at Cleveland Clinic Florida in Weston. Block openings for the 2026-27 cycle are staggered: Block 1 opened January 16, 2026, Blocks 2-8 opened February 13, 2026, and Blocks 10-13 open August 14, 2026. The $200 domestic / $400 international application fee is non-refundable. USMLE Step 1 or COMLEX Level 1 passing scores are required. As of January 1, 2026 a 10-panel drug test is required, with results sent roughly one month before start. Malpractice insurance proof is required before start. International physicians and non-US medical students with observership interest are directed separately to Cleveland Clinic's International Medical Education department.

title: University Hospitals Cleveland Visiting Medical Student Program
specialty: Multi-specialty (UHCMC, Rainbow Babies, Seidman Cancer, MacDonald Women's)
shortDescription: VSLO visiting program at UH Cleveland's four teaching hospitals for 4th-year LCME/AOA students. Also hosts the David Satcher Clerkship for US-citizen rotators.
fullDescription: University Hospitals Cleveland Medical Center hosts visiting medical students across its flagship campus and three specialty hospitals: UH Rainbow Babies & Children's Hospital, UH Seidman Cancer Center, and UH MacDonald Women's Hospital. The program accepts domestic medical students who have completed their core clinical training and will be in the fourth year of medical education at their LCME- or AOA-accredited medical school. Applications must be submitted through AAMC VSLO with 'University Hospitals Cleveland Medical Center' as host; Case Western Reserve students register through the CWRU Registrar instead. On-service responsibilities include patient histories and physicals, procedures with supervision, didactics, and department conferences. UHCMC also runs the David Satcher Clerkship for 4-8 US-citizen students from July to October. CWRU School of Medicine does not directly accept visiting students; UHCMC, Cleveland Clinic, VA Northeast Ohio, and MetroHealth collectively handle all Cleveland-area visiting applications.
